summary: Gruber examines the 'poof' animation in Mac OS X — the effect when dragging items off the Dock, Finder sidebar, or toolbars — arguing it represents genuine UI inconsistency far more important than the brushed metal debate. He traces how Apple pioneered safe deletion via the Trash, then undermined that clarity by introducing poofing as a second, inconsistent deletion gesture. The Dock's poofing prevents useful unmodified drags (like creating aliases), the Finder sidebar wastes the entire drag concept on poofing alone, and only toolbar poofing is appropriate since toolbar icons have no meaning outside the toolbar. He argues items should behave as aliases with drag-to-Trash for removal, as third-party apps like DragThing already demonstrate.
key_points: Poofing conflicts with normal drag-and-drop, preventing useful unmodified drags from the Dock and Finder sidebar
The Dock's behavior requires unintuitive Command-drag modifier keys to get useful drag functionality that should be the default
DragThing demonstrates the correct approach: treat dock items as aliases, use drag-to-Trash for removal
The Finder sidebar is even worse than the Dock — no modifier key combination allows dragging an actual reference to the item
Toolbar poofing is the only appropriate use because toolbar icons have no meaning outside the toolbar itself
money_quotes: It's a cute effect, made for demoing how cool Mac OS X looks — but it's shitty user-interface design, and Apple has implemented it inconsistently.
In the Finder sidebar, the entire concept of dragging — one of the most useful actions of the Mac's UI vocabulary — is wasted entirely on poofing.
In the old Mac OS, there was one clear way to delete something using a mouse gesture, that being to drag it to the Trash. This worked remarkably well.
You were never more than one bad wildcard away from wiping out essential files.
This is just a confusing side-effect of the Dock's conflated roles as both a launcher and process manager.
Much better would be if sidebar items were aliases. Then they could serve both as drop targets and draggable items.
Knowing this Command-drag shortcut makes the Dock much more useful. However, it's very much counter-intuitive.
key_insight: Apple's poof animation sacrifices the fundamental utility of drag-and-drop for a cute visual effect, and the solution is obvious: treat proxy icons as aliases and use drag-to-Trash for removal.
